skill.name: async-python-patterns
skill.description: Guidance for designing Python asyncio applications, including coroutine patterns, task coordination, timeouts, queues, locks, aiohttp usage, and testing practices.
skill.summary: Learn practical asyncio patterns for concurrent, non-blocking Python applications.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mostly false positives from Markdown backticks, Python code fences, and demonstration URLs. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden execution, or malicious intent was found in the reviewed SKILL.md content. A low-risk content issue remains because one educational database example uses string interpolation for SQL queries.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: The static command detections correspond to inline Markdown code spans and fenced Python examples. They do not contain shell execution instructions, subprocess calls, or user-controlled command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown formatting boundaries or Python asyncio examples. No shell command execution primitive or command injection path was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Demonstration URLs in Educational Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are example API, httpbin, and Python documentation URLs used to explain async HTTP patterns. No secrets, local files, environment variables, or user data are sent to these endpoints by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references appear only inside documentation examples and resource links. The skill contains no executable installer or runtime code that initiates network traffic.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Blocker Patterns Are Benign Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto and reconnaissance matches are benign terms inside the skill description, list comprehensions, simulated database examples, object attributes, and best-practice prose. No cryptographic operation, system inventory collection, or host reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the matched contexts shows ordinary Python tutorial content. The matches do not perform crypto, inspect host state, or collect system details.

low_findings.title: Educational SQL Example Uses String Interpolation
low_findings.description: The async database example builds SQL queries with f-strings. Although the example is simulated and the parameter is typed as an integer, copied production code should use parameterized queries.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code visibly interpolates a variable into SQL strings. Risk is limited because this is documentation-only sample code with an integer annotation and simulated database client.

dangerous_patterns.title: String-Interpolated SQL in Sample Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample database helper places a variable directly into SQL text. This is a risky pattern if copied into real database code without parameter binding.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is directly visible on the matched SQL lines. The finding is low severity because the code is educational and non-executing within the skill.

content.value_statement: Async Python code is easy to write incorrectly when tasks, timeouts, cancellation, and blocking calls interact. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for safer asyncio design and review.
actual_capabilities: Explains coroutine, task, future, async context manager, and async iterator concepts.
Provides patterns for gather, task creation, queues, semaphores, locks, and cancellation.
Shows async HTTP, web scraping, database, and WebSocket examples using simulated or common libraries.
Highlights timeouts, connection pooling, batching, and executor use for blocking work.
Covers common asyncio pitfalls and pytest-asyncio testing patterns.
limitations: It is a guidance-only Markdown skill and does not run benchmarks or inspect live services.
Several examples are simplified and require production hardening before reuse.
It does not replace library-specific documentation for FastAPI, aiohttp, asyncpg, or pytest.
It does not automatically convert synchronous applications to async architecture.

use_cases.title: Plan an Async API Service
use_cases.description: Design task boundaries, timeouts, connection pooling, and cancellation handling before implementing an async web service.

use_cases.title: Review Concurrent Python Code
use_cases.description: Check an asyncio implementation for blocking calls, missing awaits, unbounded gather usage, and weak error handling.

use_cases.title: Learn Practical Async Patterns
use_cases.description: Use focused examples to understand queues, semaphores, locks, async iterators, and test structure.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Async Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the async-python-patterns skill to explain coroutines, tasks, and await using a small Python service example.

prompt_templates.title: Design Concurrent Requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the async-python-patterns skill to design a bounded concurrent request workflow with timeouts, retries, and clear cancellation behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Async Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the async-python-patterns skill to review my asyncio code for blocking calls, missing awaits, task leaks, and unsafe gather usage.

prompt_templates.title: Architect an Async Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the async-python-patterns skill to propose an async producer-consumer pipeline with backpressure, rate limits, graceful shutdown, and test strategy.

output_examples.input: I need to fetch many APIs without overwhelming the upstream service.
output_examples.output: A bounded concurrency plan using semaphores, per-request timeouts, connection pooling, and result collection with explicit error handling.

output_examples.input: My async worker sometimes hangs during shutdown.
output_examples.output: A review checklist covering cancellation propagation, queue draining, timeout guards, task cleanup, and tests for shutdown paths.

output_examples.input: I want to teach my team asyncio patterns.
output_examples.output: A structured learning path from coroutines to tasks, queues, synchronization, aiohttp usage, and pytest-asyncio tests.

best_practices: Set explicit timeouts around external I/O and propagate cancellation cleanly.
Bound concurrency with semaphores, queues, or connection limits instead of launching unlimited tasks.
Keep blocking work out of the event loop or move it to an executor with clear limits.
anti_patterns: Calling blocking functions such as time.sleep inside async functions.
Creating tasks without awaiting, tracking, or cancelling them during shutdown.
Copying simplified SQL or HTTP examples into production without validation and parameter binding.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Python code?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guidance skill with examples for an AI assistant to reference.

faq.question: Can it help with FastAPI or aiohttp projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes async API and aiohttp patterns that can guide service design and code review.

faq.question: Does it cover cancellation and shutdown?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes cancellation handling concepts and common pitfalls for long-running async tasks.

faq.question: Can it optimize CPU-bound Python code?
faq.answer: Only partly. It explains executor use, but asyncio is mainly for I/O-bound workloads.

faq.question: Are the examples production-ready?
faq.answer: No. They are teaching examples and should be adapted with validation, parameter binding, logging, and observability.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
